review_summary: This unit supports procedural questions about nonlinear function approximation, gradients, Jacobians, and how the chain rule extends to composed vector-valued functions.

repaired_question_bank.question: Why does stacking only matrix multiplications fail to give a deep network substantially more representational power?
choices: A composition of linear transforms can collapse into one linear transform.
Matrix multiplication cannot be differentiated by gradient methods.
Linear layers cannot be implemented with vectors or tensors.
Each additional matrix forces the output dimension to be one.
repaired_question_bank.explanation: Multiple linear transforms composed together are still a linear transform. Nonlinear activation functions are what let stacked layers model more complex functions.

repaired_question_bank.question: For a scalar-valued function with many input variables, what does its gradient contain?
choices: One partial derivative with respect to each input variable.
One output value for every training example in the dataset.
The original inputs copied into a vector of the same size.
A matrix whose rows correspond to every possible model parameter.
repaired_question_bank.explanation: A gradient generalizes a single-variable derivative to many inputs. It records how the scalar output changes when each input coordinate changes.

transcript_quotes: gradient will now be a vector

repaired_question_bank.question: In multivariable calculus for neural networks, what role do Jacobians play in the chain rule?
choices: They multiply together to propagate derivatives through composed vector-valued functions.
They replace all nonlinearities with threshold functions.
They store the training data used by stochastic gradient descent.
They convert every vector-valued function into a scalar loss automatically.
repaired_question_bank.explanation: For vector-valued intermediate computations, a Jacobian stores the local partial derivatives. The chain rule combines these local derivative maps by multiplying the relevant Jacobians.
